coredata.dc:title: Characterization of aluminized layer formation during annealing of Ti coated with an Al film
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Romankov, S.
Sha, W.
Ermakov, E.
Mamaeva, A.
coredata.dc:description: During annealing of the Ti surface coated by the Al film, different aluminide phases were formed as the result of reactions between Ti and Al. The structural formation of aluminide phases and the microstructure formed after annealing treatment depended on the thickness of the initial Al film. After annealing treatment up to 900°C, the microstructure of the overlayer clearly showed contours of grain boundaries of the initial Ti substrate. The shapes, sizes and distribution of the particles in the overlayer depended on the microstructure of the initial Ti substrate as well. The microstructure of the initial Ti substrate had a great effect on the transformation kinetics. Retardation of the reaction was observed on the substrate with lamellar structure. After the elimination of the overlayer, a fine plate structure was formed. The change of the phase compositions of the overlayer led to change in the microhardness. The peak value of the microhardness was observed with the lamellar like morphology of overlayer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium
Titanium aluminides
Microstructure
Phase transformation
Coating

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical micrographs of the initial Ti substrates: (a) sample A; (b) sample B; and (c) sample C.
X-ray diffraction patterns (Cu Kα) of the Ti samples coated with Al film after annealing: (a) thickness of Al film of 2μm; and (b) thickness of Al film of 3.5μm.
X-ray diffraction patterns (Cu Kα) of the Ti samples coated with Al film with the thickness of 6μm after annealing: (a) sample A; (b) sample B; and (c) sample C.

Microstructure of the overlayer on the Ti surface after annealing: (a) sample A, 3.5μm Al film, 600°C; (b) sample C, 3.5μm Al film, 600°C; (c) sample A, 2μm Al film, 600°C; and (d) sample B, 2μm Al film, 900°C.
Detailed structure after annealing at 600°C as a function of the thickness of the Al film: (a) sample A, 2μm film; (b) sample A, 3.5μm film; (c) sample A, 6μm film; and (d) sample C, 6μm film.

Detailed structure (a), (c), (e); and Al distribution in same area, by Al Kα1 line (b), (d), (f) of sample C after annealing at 800°C as a function of the thickness of the Al film: (a) and (b) 6μm film; (c) and (d) 3.5μm film; (e) and (f) 2μm film.
Microstructure of sample C after annealing at 900°C as a function of the thickness of the Al film: (a) and (b) 6μm film; (c) and (d) 2μm film.

Microstructure after annealing at 1000°C: (a) typical structure after elimination of overlayer; and (b) structure of sample C with the thickness of Al film of 6μm where the overlayer was not eliminated yet.
Microhardness of overlayer after annealing: (a) thickness of the Al film of 3.5μm; and (b) thickness of the Al film of 6μm.
The phase composition of overlayer on the Ti surface as a function of annealing temperature, the thickness of the Al film and the grain structure of the initial substrate

article.ce:title: Characterization of aluminized layer formation during annealing of Ti coated with an Al film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics & Technology, 050032 Almaty 32, Kazakhstan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Metals Research Group, School of Planning, Architecture, and Civil Engineering, The Queen's University of Belfast, Belfast BT7 1NN, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During annealing of the Ti surface coated by the Al film, different aluminide phases were formed as the result of reactions between Ti and Al. The structural formation of aluminide phases and the microstructure formed after annealing treatment depended on the thickness of the initial Al film. After annealing treatment up to 900°C, the microstructure of the overlayer clearly showed contours of grain boundaries of the initial Ti substrate. The shapes, sizes and distribution of the particles in the overlayer depended on the microstructure of the initial Ti substrate as well. The microstructure of the initial Ti substrate had a great effect on the transformation kinetics. Retardation of the reaction was observed on the substrate with lamellar structure. After the elimination of the overlayer, a fine plate structure was formed. The change of the phase compositions of the overlayer led to change in the microhardness. The peak value of the microhardness was observed with the lamellar like morphology of overlayer.

ce:section.ce:para: Ti and its alloys have been widely used in various applications. However, their wearing behavior and corrosion resistance can be improved in order to ensure long-term durability. Surface modification is one of the effective ways to improve these properties, and coatings based on titanium aluminides can be especially effective [1–3]. In order to develop a processing strategy for protective coating based on the aluminide phases for titanium alloys it is necessary to understand the growth processes of these phases by studying kinetics of structural formation and their chemical and thermal stability. In the present work, we studied how the thickness of Al film and the structure of the initial substrate could have effect on the interdiffusion growth of aluminide phases on the Ti substrate.

The polycrystalline commercial titanium was used as substrates. The samples, cut from a plate with a thickness of 1mm were mechanically polished and then electropolished. Various microstructures of initial substrates were formed by heat treatment. Sample A consisted of irregular grains (Fig. 1a). Sample B had a coarse grain structure (Fig. 1b). Sample C had the coarse grain lamellar structure (Fig. 1c). The Al layer with thickness of about 2, 3.5 and 6μm on the Ti substrates was formed by thermal deposition of 99.999%Al in a vacuum chamber at an operating pressure of 6×10−3 Pa. The temperature of the substrates during deposition was fixed at 100°C. The as-synthesized samples were annealed successively in vacuum of 10−5 Pa at temperatures ranging between 600 and 1000°C at every 100°C and were then cooled in the furnace. In all cases, the annealing time was 2h. The resulting structure was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Cu Kα) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

Figs. 2 and 3
show the XRD patterns of samples with different thickness of the Al film after annealing at temperature range between 600 and 1000°C. Table 1 summarizes the phase composition of the overlayer formed on titanium surface during the heat treatment, as function of the thickness of the Al film and the grain structure of the initial substrate. Al reacted with Ti on heating up to 600°C forming an Al3Ti overlayer. Annealing samples with the thickness of the Al films of 2 and 3.5μm at 600°C gave two modifications of Al3Ti on the Ti surface (Fig. 2). One was the standard Al3Ti compound in the Ti-Al phase diagram. The other one was denoted as Al24Ti8 [4] with a lattice parameter c four times of the standard Al3Ti phase. The Al24Ti8 phase was not observed in the X-ray diffraction patterns of the samples with the thickness of the Al film of 6μm. Furthermore, after annealing at 600°C, the Al film on the surface of the sample C with the thickness of 6μm was not completely spent to form the Al3Ti compound (Fig. 3c). This indicates that the diffusion process was faster on the surface of samples with the polyhedral grain structures than on the samples with the lamellar structure. By examining the intensity of the diffraction lines, the volume fraction of the Al3Ti compound and hence the thicknesses of the corresponding overlayer formed on the Ti surface depended on the thickness of the initial Al film (Figs. 2 and 3).

After annealing at 600°C the microstructure of the overlayer clearly showed contours of grain boundaries of the initial Ti substrates (Fig. 4 ). It was particularly apparent in the case of the samples with the thickness of the Al film of 3.5μm (Fig. 4a and b). Independently of the structure of the initial substrate, the degradation of microstructure was observed on the samples with 2μm film (Fig. 4c). The structural degradation was not observed on the samples with the thickness of the Al films of 3.5 and 6μm. By increasing the annealing temperature, the disappearance of the overlayer took place on these degraded parts first (Fig. 4d). The structural degradation of the thin overlayer formed during annealing was possibly related with the distribution of stresses between substrate and the Al film, which in turn had a great effect on the diffusion fluxes during subsequent annealing.

Fig. 5a–c show the detailed structure of the overlayers after annealing at 600°C as a function of the thickness of the Al film, by the example of sample A. The structure of the overlayer of the sample with the thickness of the Al film of 3.5μm consisted of the equiaxed particles with the size of about 0.4μm (Fig. 5b). For the sample with 6μm film, the particles were finer (Fig. 5c). It indicates that the elastic strains in the overlayer for the sample with the thicker film were higher. For the sample with 2μm film, after annealing at 600°C, coalescence of the particles particularly close to the degraded areas was observed (Fig. 5a). Fig. 5d shows the remains of the Al film on the surface of sample C with the thickness of the Al film of 6μm.

After annealing at 600°C, the samples were annealed at 700°C. During annealing at 700° C, the Al3Ti and Al24Ti8 compounds started decomposing. On the surface of the samples with the thickness of the Al films of 2 and 3.5μm, the Al2Ti and TiAl phases were formed. The weak superstructure line (101) at 2θ =26° indicated the presence of the Ti3Al compound in the overlayers. The diffraction lines of the Al24Ti8 phase were observed in the X-ray diffraction patterns of all samples with 3.5μm film and only in the one of sample C with 2μm film. In the case of the samples with the thickness of the Al film of 6μm, the Al3Ti lines and the weak Al2Ti peaks were detected in the X-ray diffraction patterns after annealing at 700°C (Table 1).

After subsequent annealing at 800°C, on the surface of the samples with the thickness of the Al film of 2μm, the Ti3Al compound was formed. In addition, the new diffraction lines with d =2.48, 2.15, 1.51, 1.29Å were detected in the X-ray diffraction patterns (Fig. 2). In Table 1, Figs. 2 and 3, this phase was denoted as X-phase. The mechanism of its formation is considered further below. The annealing treatment of the samples with 3.5μm film at 800°C led to the formation of the Ti3Al and TiAl compounds (Table 1, Fig. 2). Annealing samples with 6μm film at 800°C gave Al2Ti and TiAl on the surface (Fig. 3). By comparing the intensities of the diffraction lines from the Al2Ti and TiAl compounds, the maximum volume fraction of Al2Ti was observed on the surface of sample B, and the TiAl compound on sample A. The weak superstructure line (101) of the Ti3Al phase was observed in the X-ray diffraction patterns of samples A and B and it was not detected in the one of sample C. Furthermore, in the X-ray diffraction pattern of sample C with the thickness of the Al film of 6μm, the Al3Ti peaks were observed (Fig. 3). Annealing of sample C at 800°C did not lead to complete decomposition of the Al3Ti phase.

Fig. 6 shows the detailed structure of overlayers after annealing at 800°C as a function of the thickness of the Al film, by the example of sample C. After annealing at 800°C, the overlayer structure of sample C with 6μm film consisted of polyhedral particles with the sides of 0.8–1.5μm (Fig. 6a). The particles formed colonies along the lamellar boundaries of the initial substrate in the way that they showed the microstructure of the initial ones. In the case of the sample with the thickness of the Al film of 3.5μm, the leveling of the lamellar like morphology and coalescence of the particles took place after annealing at 800°C (Fig. 6c). By examining the micrograph of the sample with the thickness of the Al film of 2μm, it can be seen that the process of the particle coalescence led to the formation of the hillock morphology (Fig. 6e). Fig. 6b, d, and f show the corresponding Al distribution measured on surface according to the Al Kα1 line. For the three cases, Al appears to have distributed differently on the surface. For the sample with the thickness of the Al film of 6μm, more Al was observed around lamellar boundaries. For the sample with 3.5μm film, Al mainly concentrated on the remnants of the lamellars; and for the sample with 2μm film, Al uniformly distributed on the surface. The Al distribution on surface as a function of phase composition of overlayer could show its migration in the process of structural formation, although these elemental mapping results should be treated with caution due to the uneven nature of the surface possibly leading to experimental errors.

This tendency in the microstructural evolution as a function of the thickness of the Al film was observed also on samples A and B. For the Al film of 6μm, the particles kept their form after 800°C annealing, while for the Al film of 2μm, coalescence of the particles and the formation of the hillock morphology was observed after annealing at 600°C. Coalescence of the particles was likely to be related with recrystallization of the overlayers. Nucleation and growth of new phases led to the changes in microstructure. The distinctions between the structures of samples A, B and C were the detailed forms and sizes of particles, the temperature range of their formation, and morphology of their distribution on the surface. For instance, on sample A with 6μm film, the particles had platelike form with sides of about 0.3μm (Fig. 5c) and they formed on heating to 600°C. For sample C with 6μm film, the particles had polyhedral form with the sides of 0.8–1.5μm (Fig. 6a) and they completely formed only after heating treatment at 700°C. These results signify that the interdiffusion growth of aluminized overlayers depended on the structure of initial substrate as well as the thickness of the Al film.

After subsequent increasing of annealing temperature to 900°C, the intensity of the X-phase diffraction lines increased while the intensity of the Ti3Al ones decreased in the X-ray diffraction patterns of the samples with the thickness of Al film of 2μm. The annealing treatment of samples with 3.5μm film at 900°C gave the X-phase and the Ti3Al compound as well (Table 1, Fig. 2). The shape of diffractions lines and their intensities in the X-ray diffraction patterns of samples A and C with 2 and 3.5μm film indicated differences in the kinetics of the reaction after annealing at 900°C (Fig. 2). In the case of 6μm film, the X-phase and Ti3Al were formed only on the surface of sample A. In the X-ray diffraction patterns of samples B and C, the Ti3Al and TiAl peaks were detected (Fig. 3). Judging from intensities of the diffraction lines from the Ti3Al and TiAl phases the maximum volume fraction of TiAl was observed on the surface of sample C, and the Ti3Al compound on sample A. This means that the rate of reaction on the surface of sample A was much higher than on the surface of samples B and C. At temperatures about 880°C, α (hcp)→β (bcc) transformation of pure Ti takes place. Upon heating to 900°C, the nucleation and growth of the β-phase could depend on the structure of the initial substrate. The varied growth of the β-grains was likely to have a great effect on the transformation kinetics of the overlayers on the Ti surface.

Fig. 7 shows the microstructure of the overlayer after annealing at 900°C as a function of the thickness of the Al film, by the example of sample C. On the surface of the sample with the thickness of the Al film of 6μm, the lamellar like morphology, which had clearly shown contours of grain boundaries of the initial Ti substrate (Fig. 4b), was in the process of decomposition (Fig. 7a). The particles, which were observed after annealing at 800°C (Fig. 6a), entirely coalesced and formed continuous overlayer (Fig. 7b). After annealing at 900°C the sample with the thickness of the Al film of 2μm, the lamellar like morphology was completely decomposed and a porous structure was formed (Fig. 7c and d). The decomposition of the lamellar like morphology was likely related with changes of the morphology of the grain structure of the initial Ti substrate. Thicker overlayer required more time for its recrystallization and microstructural transformation. Similar changes in the structure happened on samples A and B. There was a spectrum of the detailed structures observed after annealing at 900°C between what are shown in Fig. 8b and d. The continuous structure was typical for the two-phase TiAl+Ti3Al overlayer, formed from the 6μm films. The porous one was typical for the Ti3Al+X-phase overlayer, formed from the films with thickness less than 6μm.

After annealing at 1000°C, the diffraction lines of pure Ti were detected in the X-ray diffraction patterns of samples A and B with the thickness of the Al films of 2 and 3.5μm. The peaks of the X-phase and Ti3Al were observed in the ones of samples C (Table 1, Fig. 2). In the case of 6μm films, the diffraction lines of pure Ti were observed in the X-ray diffraction pattern of sample B. The peaks of the X-phase and pure Ti were detected in the one of sample A. The peaks of X-phase and Ti3Al were observed in the one of sample C (Fig. 3). The diffraction lines with d =2.48, 2.40, 2.15, 2.08, 1.51, 1.29Å were related to the X-phase. These lines could not be attributed to the known titanium aluminide phases according to the Power Diffraction Standards. However, some of these lines could be attributed to some oxide and carbide phases. It is necessary to note that these diffraction lines were not detected in the X-ray diffraction patterns when we studied the underside of samples. It means that formation of the X-phase was related with the Al thin film.

The microstructure formed after the elimination of the overlayer in the favor of releasing Al into Ti consisted of very coarse grains. The inner structure of the grains consisted of very fine plates with different crystallographic orientation in the adjoining grains (Fig. 8a). The chemical composition of these grains was Ti–2.3%Al. The fine plate microstructure was also observed on the underside of the samples. That structure looks like the deformation relief. It was not observed after annealing pure Ti without Al film at 1000°C. It means that formation of fine plate microstructure was related with the Al thin film and overlayer formed on the surface during heat treatment. Then, it can be assumed that upon heating above 900°C, the structure of the substrate consisted of the coarse β-grains (bcc lattice) and the overlayer consisted of Ti3Al (DO22 structure). Because of the crystallographic differences at the interface a strong deformation stress appeared, which led to the formation of the fine plate structure during subsequent cooling.

Fig. 8b shows the microstructure of overlayer on sample C with the 6μm film after annealing at 1000°C, where the maximum volume fraction of the X-phase was observed. Microanalysis of that structure showed the presence of oxygen and carbon. However, it was difficult to determine the accurate chemical composition by the means of X-ray emission analysis because of multilayered structure. Taking into account the presence of oxygen and carbon in the overlayer, we suggest the following explanation for the formation of the X-phase. The formation of the X-phase was not related to some fixed temperature. In all cases, the diffraction lines of the X-phase were detected only along with the Ti3Al ones (Table 1), which was after the increase of Ti concentration in the overlayer. After the elimination of the overlayer, microanalysis did not give the presence of oxygen and carbon on the Ti surface. On the undersides of the samples, the X-phase was not formed either. It could mean that the residual gas in furnace during annealing did not have a great effect on the formation of that phase. The formation of X-phase was only concerned with the Al thin film. Consequently, it can be assumed that during thermal deposition of Al films the atoms of the residual gases in the evacuated chamber were likely to penetrate the film. The thicker Al film resulted in more gas atoms in the film. When heating with increasing of the Ti concentration in the overlayer, the oxide and carbide phases started forming. At 1000°C, when the diffusion flux directed towards the substrate and the elimination of the aluminized overlayer took place, the oxygen and carbon atoms were released, and the X-phase disappeared. On the other hand, if oxide or carbide phases formed after annealing treatment at 900°C, it was unlikely for them to disappear completely after treatment at 1000°C. It means that the X-phase could be also some metastable phase formed during the last stage of diffusion cementation. For instance, the Ti3.3Al phase has the lattice plane spacing d =2.47, 2.37, 2.18, 1.51, 1.31Å.

Fig. 9 shows the microhardness of the samples with the thickness of the Al film of 3.5 and 6μm. The relationship between the microhardness and the phase composition was observed. The change of the phase compositions led to change in the microhardness. Higher values of the microhardness were observed for the samples with the lamellar like morphology of overlayer. The microhardness increased when on the samples with 6μm film two-phase Al3Ti+Al2Ti overlayer was formed. It decreased after formation of multiphase composition. For all samples, the microhardness started increasing significantly when the Ti-rich aluminides were formed on the surface. After the elimination of the overlayer, the microhardness decreased.

ce:list-item.ce:para: During annealing of the Ti surface coated by Al films, different aluminide phases were formed as the result of reactions between Ti and Al. The structural formation of aluminide phases and the microstructure of the overlayer formed after annealing treatment depended on the thickness of the initial Al film. The thicker Al films led to the thicker overlayers and more time was needed for their diffusion cementation and recrystallization. Annealing at the temperature of 1000°C resulted in the elimination of the overlayer in favor of releasing Al into Ti.

After annealing treatment up to 900°C, the microstructure of the overlayer clearly revealed contours of grain boundaries of the initial Ti substrate. After annealing at 900°C, the contour morphology started changing. The shapes, sizes and distribution of the particles in the overlayer depended on the microstructure of the initial Ti substrate, which had a great effect on the transformation behavior. The retardation of the reactions was observed on the substrate with lamellar structure. After the elimination of the overlayer, a fine plate structure was formed.
The change of the phase compositions of the overlayer led to change in the microhardness. Higher values of the microhardness were observed with the lamellar like morphology of overlayer.
